text: Bit Manipulation Pattern: Check if bit k is set. Use (n >> k) & 1. Returns 1 if bit k is set, 0 otherwise. Alternative: n & (1 << k) != 0. Use when testing individual bits in flags, bitmasks, or encoded states. Time: O(1). Example: check if number is odd with n & 1.

Bit Manipulation Pattern: Set bit k. Use n | (1 << k). Forces bit k to 1 without affecting others. Use when turning on specific flags or recording presence of element k in a bitmask set. Time: O(1). Complement: clear bit k with n & ~(1 << k), toggle with n ^ (1 << k).

Bit Manipulation Pattern: Count set bits (popcount). Brian Kernighan's trick: while n: count+=1; n &= n-1. Each iteration removes the lowest set bit. Time: O(number of set bits). For O(1): use bin(n).count('1') or hardware popcount. Use for: Hamming weight, counting subsets, checking power of 2 (popcount == 1).

Bit Manipulation Pattern: Find single number (XOR trick). XOR of a number with itself is 0. XOR with 0 gives the number itself. XOR is commutative and associative. To find one unique number among pairs: XOR all numbers, pairs cancel out. Time: O(n), Space: O(1). Extends to: find two unique numbers by splitting on a differing bit.

Bit Manipulation Pattern: Power of two check. A power of 2 has exactly one bit set. Check: n > 0 and (n & (n-1)) == 0. n & (n-1) clears the lowest set bit; if result is 0, only one bit was set. Use for: alignment checks, hash table sizes, fast modulo (n % power2 == n & (power2-1)). Time: O(1).

Bit Manipulation Pattern: Isolate lowest set bit. Expression: n & (-n) or n & (~n+1). Gets the value of the rightmost set bit. Use in Fenwick tree updates/queries, Gosper's hack for iterating subsets. Example: 12 (1100) -> 4 (0100). Complement: n & (n-1) removes the lowest set bit. Time: O(1).

Bit Manipulation Pattern: Bitmask DP for subset enumeration. Represent set of n items as integer bitmask (0 to 2^n-1). State: dp[mask] = result for subset encoded in mask. Transition: for each bit in mask, try including/excluding. Use for: TSP (traveling salesman), assignment problems, subsets with constraints. Time: O(2^n * n), Space: O(2^n).

Bit Manipulation Pattern: Enumerate all subsets of a mask. Template: sub = mask; while sub > 0: process(sub); sub = (sub-1) & mask. This iterates through all submasks in decreasing order. Total iterations across all masks is 3^n (each element can be: in neither, in mask only, or in submask). Use for: sum over subsets DP. Time: O(3^n) total.

Bit Manipulation Pattern: XOR for find missing or duplicate. Find missing number in [0..n]: XOR all indices 0..n with all array values; missing number remains. Detect duplicate: XOR all with all [1..n]; surviving value is the duplicate. Time: O(n), Space: O(1). Works because all numbers except the answer cancel out via XOR.

Bit Manipulation Pattern: Swap without temporary variable. a ^= b; b ^= a; a ^= b. After: a has original b, b has original a. Caution: fails if a and b are same memory location (a ^= a gives 0). Rarely useful in practice but demonstrates XOR properties. Time: O(1). Also: swap using arithmetic a=a+b; b=a-b; a=a-b (overflow risk).

Bit Manipulation Pattern: Reverse bits. Iteratively extract LSB and shift into result. Template: result=0; for _ in range(32): result=(result<<1)|(n&1); n>>=1. Or divide and conquer: swap halves of 16 bits, then 8, 4, 2, 1. Use for: reversing bit representation, mirror image problems. Time: O(log bits) with divide-and-conquer.

Bit Manipulation Pattern: Multiply/divide by powers of 2. Left shift (<<k) multiplies by 2^k. Right shift (>>k) divides by 2^k (floor for positive). Arithmetic right shift (>>) preserves sign bit. Use for fast multiplication/division in performance-critical code. Example: mid = lo + (hi-lo)>>1. Caution: overflow with large left shifts.

Bit Manipulation Pattern: Gray code. n-th Gray code = n ^ (n >> 1). Consecutive Gray codes differ by exactly one bit. Use for: error correction, rotary encoders, generating Hamiltonian paths on hypercubes. Inverse: decode Gray code g by cascading XOR from MSB. Applications in generating minimal-change sequences. Time: O(1) per value.

Bit Manipulation Pattern: Count bits from 0 to n (DP). dp[i] = dp[i >> 1] + (i & 1). Each number i has one more bit than i>>1 if LSB is 1, else same. Precompute all bit counts 0..n in O(n) time. Use for: counting set bits in ranges, prefix sum of bit counts. Time: O(n), Space: O(n).

Bit Manipulation Pattern: Bitwise AND of numbers in range. Result of AND from m to n. Key insight: AND decreases as range widens; find common prefix of m and n in binary. Right-shift both until m==n, count shifts, left-shift result back. Trailing bits become 0 because some bit toggles within range. Time: O(log n).

Bit Manipulation Pattern: Maximum XOR in array (Trie). Build binary trie from all numbers. For each number, traverse trie greedily choosing opposite bit to maximize XOR. Time: O(n * 32). Alternatively, prefix XOR + greedy for max XOR subarray. For max XOR of two numbers in array, sort and compare adjacent numbers is wrong; trie is correct approach.

Bit Manipulation Pattern: Check if two integers have opposite signs. (x ^ y) < 0 is true if signs differ (sign bits differ). Avoids branch and handles edge cases cleanly. Also: check if number is negative with n >> 31 (gives -1 or 0 for 32-bit). Use in: signed/unsigned comparisons, overflow detection. Time: O(1).

Bit Manipulation Pattern: Subset sum with bitmask. Check if subset of array sums to target. State: bitmask of included elements. Or DP where each bit represents a possible sum. For small n (<=20): iterate all 2^n subsets with bitmask. For larger: meet-in-the-middle splitting into two halves of 2^(n/2). Time: O(2^n) or O(2^(n/2) log).

Bit Manipulation Pattern: Fast set operations with integers. Represent set S of elements from universe U={0..n-1} as bitmask. Union: a|b, Intersection: a&b, Difference: a&~b, Complement: ~a&((1<<n)-1), Membership: (a>>x)&1, Insert x: a|(1<<x), Remove x: a&~(1<<x). Much faster than hash sets for small universes. Time: O(1) per operation.

Bit Manipulation Pattern: Detect if XOR of segment equals 0 (prefix XOR). Build prefix XOR array: px[i] = nums[0]^...^nums[i-1]. XOR of segment [l,r] = px[r+1]^px[l]. If px[r+1]==px[l], XOR is 0. Finding subarrays with even number of each bit, or partitioning into equal XOR parts. Time: O(n) preprocessing, O(1) per query.
